mnemonics.title: Shader Stage Order
mnemonics.content: VAPGTF: Vertex → Assembly → Primitive → Geometry → Tessellation → Fragment (remember: Very Angry Penguins Grew Tired Fishing)

mnemonics.title: Pipeline Flow
mnemonics.content: V-I-P-R-F-B: Vertex Input → Process → Rasterize → Fragment → Blend (Video In Pubs Run Fast Bouncing)

mnemonics.title: Vertex Shader Purpose
mnemonics.content: VAP: Vertex position transformation, Attribute interpolation setup, Per-vertex lighting calculation

acronyms.usage: High-level C-like language for writing shaders in OpenGL

acronyms.expansion: General-Purpose Computing on Graphics Processing Units
acronyms.usage: Using GPU for non-graphics parallel computations
Device-independent coordinate system after vertex transformation

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an assembly line in a factory: raw vertices enter → get transformed → assembled into shapes → broken into pixels → colored → displayed. Each station (shader) can be customized by writing new instructions.

Think of vertex shader as processing 'corners' of triangles (where they are), and fragment shader as painting the 'inside' (what color they are). Vertex determines position, fragment determines appearance.
Imagine looking through a dirty windshield - closer objects (lower depth) block view of objects behind. The depth buffer records the 'closest' object at each pixel position.
Like projecting a slide onto a screen - the continuous triangle shape gets converted into discrete pixels (dots) that can be colored individually.

analogies.analogy: Like moving from a fixed-function camera (with preset modes) to a DSLR where you can control every setting and lens for creative control.
Each shader stage is like a specialized worker station in a factory - vertex workers shape the product, fragment workers paint it, geometry workers can add/remove parts.
Like a paint-by-number system where each numbered area (fragment) gets its designated color from the palette (texture/lighting calculation).
Like settings dials on a machine that remain fixed while the machine runs - all workers see the same dial settings during one production run (draw call).
